definition: A fetish is a made object believed to house or channel power — a crafted thing venerated for the spirit or force within it. It differs from a worn *amulet* or *talisman* in the weight of belief it carries: the fetish is not merely charged but treated as itself potent, an object inhabited by power.
history: From Portuguese *feitiço*, 'charm,' from Latin *facticius*, 'made by art' (from *facere*, 'to make'). Portuguese sailors coined the term for the West African power-objects they encountered, naming them for their made-ness. The word then traveled through European thought, each discipline borrowing it in turn — anthropology for ritual objects, Marx for the commodity, Freud for desire — so that European writers even applied it back onto their own past, calling holy relics wonder-working fetishes.

def_sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
def_sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

def_sources.label: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
def_sources.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

commentary: No word better exposes how a category can be manufactured at a border. *Fetish* was not an African term but a coinage of Portuguese traders on the West African coast, systematized into a theory of primitive religion by Charles de Brosses and then annexed by the metropolitan disciplines — Marx for the *commodity*, Freud for desire. William Pietz's *The Problem of the Fetish* is the decisive intervention: the concept, he argues, was born in the intercultural trade encounter, not in Africa, and the scholarly habit of naming other people's objects 'fetishes' says more about European anxieties than about the objects.

commentary_sources.label: William Pietz, 'The Problem of the Fetish'
commentary_sources.abstract: This is the first installment of a three-part study tracing how the idea of a 'fetish' (an object believed to hold magical power) was invented by European traders describing West African religious objects in the 1500s-1600s, and how that idea later got applied, often unfairly, to describe other cultures' beliefs.

commentary_sources.label: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
commentary_sources.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

commentary_sources.label: Brian Copenhaver, Magic and the Dignity of Man
commentary_sources.abstract: Copenhaver challenges the popular idea that Renaissance philosopher Pico della Mirandola's famous speech was a humanist manifesto about human free will and dignity; he argues the text was really about magic and Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah), and that its modern reputation as being about "the dignity of man" is a five-century-long case of mistaken memory.

etymology.origin: Portuguese feitiço 'charm', from Latin facticius 'made by art' (facere 'to make')
etymology.note: Portuguese sailors coined it for West African power-objects; anthropology, Marx, and Freud each borrowed it in turn.
etymology.hook: A colonial word for 'made power' that psychology stole.

flavor_snippets.html: Some Krovikans find strength in the ears and eyes of their victims and wear such <mark>fetishes</mark> into battle.
